help.title: How to use Loan Restructuring
help.intro: Three managed actions that go beyond recording a payment: re-amortize a struggling loan, advance more funds, or permanently close an uncollectible account — all with automatic General Ledger postings and a full audit trail.

steps.title: 1. Find the loan
steps.text: Enter the Loan ID (the number shown in the loan list) and click Find Loan. The loan's current balance, status and next due date are displayed.

steps.title: 2. Reschedule (struggling borrower)
steps.text: Switch to the Reschedule tab. Enter the new term in months, repayment frequency and an optional interest rate. Click Reschedule Loan — the system deletes unpaid future installments and re-builds them over the outstanding balance. No cash moves, so no GL entry is posted.
steps.example: Borrower has 8 months left but can't meet TZS 120,000/month. Set term to 16 months → installment drops to ~TZS 60,000.

steps.title: 3. Top-Up (advance more funds)
steps.text: Switch to the Top-Up tab. Enter the amount to advance, the payment method, and a new repayment term. The system posts Dr Loans Receivable / Cr Cash (or Bank) and rebuilds the schedule over the new combined balance.
steps.example: Outstanding balance TZS 300,000 + TZS 200,000 top-up = TZS 500,000 re-amortized over 12 months.

steps.title: 4. Write-Off (uncollectible debt)
steps.text: Switch to the Write-Off tab. Enter the reason (required for audit). The system absorbs the balance against the Allowance for Loan Losses (built up by monthly provisioning), charges any shortfall to the provision expense, and closes the loan as written_off.
steps.example: Write-off reason: 'Borrower deceased — confirmed by family 2026-06-15'.

steps.title: 5. Review history
steps.text: Every action appears in the Lifecycle History table below the loan card — type, amounts, who performed it, and notes.

help.tip: Write-off is permanent and immediately removes the balance from Loans Receivable. If there is any chance of future recovery, use Reschedule instead.

help.title: How to use the Cashier Till
help.intro: Every cashier opens one till session at the start of the shift and closes it at the end. The system automatically tracks cash in (repayments you record as 'cash') and cash out (cash disbursements you process) — the physical count at close reveals any shortage or overage.

steps.title: 1. Open the drawer
steps.text: At the start of your shift, enter the cash float you are starting with (the amount physically in the drawer right now) and click Open Drawer.
steps.example: Opening float: TZS 100,000 — the float received from yesterday's close or petty cash.

steps.title: 2. Transact normally
steps.text: Record cash repayments and process cash disbursements through the normal loan pages — the till tracks them automatically. You don't need to come back here during the shift.

steps.title: 3. Check the board mid-shift
steps.text: Refresh this page any time to see the live position: opening float + cash in (collections) − cash out (disbursements) = expected amount in the drawer right now.

steps.title: 4. Count and close
steps.text: At the end of the shift, physically count the cash in the drawer. Enter the exact count in the Close Till box and click Count & Close Drawer.
steps.example: Expected TZS 340,000, you count TZS 338,500 → variance = −TZS 1,500 (shortage — investigate before locking up).

steps.title: 5. Review history
steps.text: The Session History table shows all your past sessions. Admins see all cashiers. A zero variance means the drawer balanced perfectly.

help.tip: Close the till every day even if there are no transactions — a zero-transaction close confirms the drawer was balanced and undisturbed.

help.title: How to use Loan Settings
help.intro: These three rates are the system-wide defaults used every time a loan is calculated, a penalty is charged, or a processing fee is deducted at disbursement. Change them here and every future calculation picks up the new value instantly — existing loan schedules are not retroactively changed.

steps.title: 1. Penalty rate
steps.text: The percentage applied to the overdue installment amount when a borrower is late. Used by the Overdue Management module and printed on guarantor SMS reminders.
steps.example: Set to 2.0% → a borrower with TZS 50,000 overdue owes TZS 1,000 as a late-payment penalty.

steps.title: 2. Default interest rate
steps.text: The monthly interest rate applied to new loans when the loan officer does not set a specific rate during the application — common for standard products.
steps.example: Set to 3.0% per month → a TZS 1,000,000 loan accrues TZS 30,000 interest in the first month.

steps.title: 3. Processing fee rate
steps.text: The percentage deducted from the gross loan amount at disbursement as an origination fee. Flows to Fee Income in the General Ledger.
steps.example: Set to 1.5% → a TZS 500,000 loan has TZS 7,500 withheld; the borrower receives TZS 492,500.

steps.title: 4. Save
steps.text: Click Save Settings — the new rates take effect immediately. A confirmation shows the change was applied.

help.tip: Loan officers can override the default interest rate on individual applications. These settings are the fallback when no specific rate is entered.
